# Dart PocketBase CLI: Persisted Session with authRefresh

## Goal
Build a standalone Dart CLI at `/home/user/myproject` that authenticates against the local PocketBase v0.31.0 server (`http://127.0.0.1:8090`, `users` collection), persists the resulting auth token to a `session.json` file in the current working directory, and on subsequent runs refreshes that token using the official `pocketbase` pub package.

A seed user is already provisioned: `user@example.com` / `password`.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Entrypoint: `bin/app.dart`, runnable via `dart run bin/app.dart ...` from `/home/user/myproject`.
- `dart run bin/app.dart login <email> <password>`
  - Writes `session.json` (in the current working directory) containing a non-empty PocketBase auth token.
  - Exits 0.
- `dart run bin/app.dart refresh`
  - Loads `session.json`, calls `authRefresh()` on the `users` collection, and persists the new token back to `session.json` if it changed.
  - Stdout prints exactly two lines and nothing else: the authenticated user record id, then the token's `exp` claim as an integer epoch (seconds).
  - Exits 0.
- `dart run bin/app.dart refresh` with a corrupted `session.json` (not valid JSON or missing the token):
  - Prints exactly `INVALID_SESSION` to stderr.
  - Exits 1.
I'll start by exploring the existing project structure and understanding the PocketBase Dart package API.

input.query: dart pocketbase pub.dev package authRefresh API

content.text:
# pocketbase library - Dart API - Pub.dev
created: undefined, last updated: 2026-06-10, [Read more](https://pub.dev/documentation/quickpocketbase/latest/pocketbase)

# How to keep a user signed in after auth · pocketbase ... - GitHub
created: 2023-03-25, last updated: 2025-10-24, [Read more](https://github.com/pocketbase/pocketbase/discussions/2154)

There is no such option.
...
Once again, PocketBase doesn't have the concept of access+refresh token because it doesn't need it for the reasons explained above.
A long lived Firebase refresh token serves the same purpose as long-lived PocketBase auth token.
...
Also note that the latest Dart SDK comes with a helper
```
AsyncAuthStore
```
that should help in reducing the required boilerplate to integrate with various async storage mechanisms.
You could find an example in https://github.com/pocketbase/dart-sdk?tab=readme-ov-file#authstore.
...
My understanding is that providers are only used for signing up/in a user to PocketBase.
A token created by PocketBase (not by the provider) is used from that point onwards.
It has all necessary info like the the expiry date in its payload because authentications in PocketBase is stateless, and there are no such things as an access token and a refresh token.
So a user session is only active while the token is valid, and thus a user needs to refresh the session before expiration.
Refreshing is a process only within PocketBase, without providers involved.

...
Authenticate a single auth collection record with OAuth2
**without custom redirects, deeplinks or even page reload**.
...
- Authenticate an auth record with an OAuth2 client provider and returns a new auth token and record data (including the OAuth2 user profile).

# PocketBase re-authenticate user - Stack Overflow
created: 2023-03-31, last updated: 2025-05-21, [Read more](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/75895859/pocketbase-re-authenticate-user)

- Refreshing tokens should happen while previous token is still valid.
Once a token expires, you should require user to relogin.
...
You need to add the current user token in the authRefresh()
import 'package:pocketbase/pocketbase.dart';
final pb = PocketBase('YOUR POCKET BASE URL');
final authData = await pb.collection('users').authRefresh();
```
Requires record Authorization:TOKEN header
```
The constructor :
package:pocketbase/src/services/record_service.dart Future authRefresh({ String?
expand, String?
fields, Map<String, dynamic> body = const {}, Map<String, dynamic> query = const {}, **Map<String, String> headers = const {}**, })
...
You can solve this problem by setting the value 0.95 to the **maxChildSize** property and also in the ***showModalBottomSheet*** function Set the **clipBehavior** property to **Clip.none**.
```
showModalBottomSheet(
clipBehavior: Clip.none,
builder: (context) => DraggableScrollableSheet(
maxChildSize: 0.95
)
)
```

# pocketbase_utils | Dart package - Pub.dev
created: 2025-02-12, last updated: 2026-03-28, [Read more](https://pub.dev/packages/pocketbase_utils)

pocketbase_utils is a dart library that generates Dart models code from a PocketBase collections scheme json file.
...
Dart package that creates a binding between your pocketbase server and your Flutter app by generating typesafe boilerplate code of your collections from
```
pb_schema.json
```
file.
...
### 2. Install the package #
Add the package into your
```
pubspec.yaml
```
```
...
dev_dependencies:
 ...
 pocketbase_utils: x.x.x
```
You also may have to install the json_serializable, equatable, collection, and pocketbase packages.
...
Add package configuration to your
```
pubspec.yaml
```
file. Here is a full configuration for the package:
```
---
pocketbase_utils:
...
In the root of your flutter project run:
```
dart run pocketbase_utils:generate
```
This will produce files inside
```
lib/generated/pocketbase
```
directory.
You can also change the output folder to a custom directory by adding the
```
output_dir
```
line in your
```
pubspec.yaml
```
...
### Publish package #
```
dart pub publish --dry-run
```
...

# Introduction - Authentication - Docs - PocketBase
created: undefined, last updated: 2026-06-15, [Read more](https://pocketbase.io/docs/authentication/)

`import 'package:pocketbase/pocketbase.dart';
final pb = PocketBase('http://127.0.0.1:8090');
...### Auth token verification
PocketBase doesn't have a dedicated token verification endpoint, but if you want to verify an existing
auth token from a 3rd party app you can send an
Auth refresh call, aka.
`pb.collection("users").authRefresh()`.
On valid token - it returns a new token with refreshed `exp` claim and the latest user data.
Otherwise - returns an error response.
Note that calling `authRefresh` doesn't invalidate previously issued tokens and you can safely disregard
the new one if you don't need it (as mentioned in the beginning - PocketBase doesn't store the tokens on the
server).
Performance wise, the used `HS256` algorithm for generating the JWT has very little to no
impact and it is essentially the same in terms of response time as calling
`getOne("USER_ID")` *(see
benchmarks ) *.

Title: RecordService class - pocketbase library

URL Source: https://pub.dev/documentation/pocketbase/latest/pocketbase/RecordService-class.html

Markdown Content:
The service that handles the **Record APIs**.

Usually shouldn't be initialized manually and instead [PocketBase.collection("COLLECTION")](https://pub.dev/documentation/pocketbase/latest/pocketbase/PocketBase/collection.html) should be used.

input.query: dart decode JWT token exp claim without verification base64

content.text:
# How to Decode a JWT Token Online — Jsonic Guide
created: 2026-05-11, last updated: 2026-06-06, [Read more](https://jsonic.io/guides/decode-jwt)

A JWT has 3 dot-separated base64url-encoded parts: header, payload, and signature.
...
…
...
Decoding is not verification — anyone with the token can read the payload; the 256-byte RS256 signature only proves the token was not tampered with.
...
…
). For most tokens this doesn't matter, but strictly you should replace those characters first:
```
function decodeJwtPart(part) {
const base64 = part.replace(/-/g, '+').replace(/_/g, '/')
return JSON.parse(atob(base64))
```
...
Paste any JWT token into Jsonic's JWT Decoder to instantly see the header, payload, and expiry time formatted as human-readable JSON and dates.
No server.
No uploads.
...
Split on dots, base64url-decode the first two parts, and parse as JSON:
...
Decoding unpacks the base64url encoding to read the payload — anyone can do this.
Verifying checks the signature using the secret or public key to confirm the token is authentic and unmodified.
Never trust claims without server-side verification.
...
Header (algorithm and token type), Payload (claims: sub, exp, iat, custom fields), and Signature (computed by the server).

# How can I extract expire time from jwt token in Dart?
created: 2020-04-06, last updated: 2025-03-17, [Read more](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/61055632/how-can-i-extract-expire-time-from-jwt-token-in-dart)

An elegant solution would be using the
`jwt_decoder`package.
https://pub.dev/packages/jwt_decoder
`flutter pub add jwt_decoder`
`import 'package:jwt_decoder/jwt_decoder.dart';`
or check only the expiration if that's the case:
You can easily use the
`jwt_decode`package.
following is package URL https://pub.dev/packages/jwt_decode
An alternative solution if you want to use a package:
Install corsac_jwt: https://pub.dev/packages/corsac_jwt#-installing-tab-
You can do it by decoding it, Speaking in general, JWT token conssist two part (objects), in the above JWT the result of decoding it is :
So the expire date is a timestamp (1586763136) which stand for Monday, April 13, 2020 7:32:16 AM.
...
You should use dart:convert.
With utf8 you will decode base64 and with json get Map object to call
`["exp"]`property
I solved it without using the package.
The package hasn't been updated for the last 3 years (https://pub.dev/packages/jwt_decode).

# JWT Decoder - Decode JSON Web Tokens Online | JSONLint
created: undefined, last updated: 2026-05-29, [Read more](https://jsonlint.com/jwt-decoder)

Decode and inspect JSON Web Tokens.
Paste your JWT below to view the header and payload:
...
|``` exp ```|Expiration|When the token expires (Unix timestamp)|
...
**Important:** This tool decodes JWTs but does not verify signatures.
Anyone can decode a JWT and read its contents — the signature only prevents tampering.
...
### JavaScript
```
// Decode without verifying (like this tool)
function decodeJWT(token) {
const payload = token.split('.')[1];
return JSON.parse(atob(payload));
// With a library (recommended for verification)
import jwt from 'jsonwebtoken';
const decoded = jwt.verify(token, secretKey);
```
### Python
```
import jwt
# Decode without verifying
decoded = jwt.decode(token, options={"verify_signature": False})
...
```
...
Yes, decoding is safe.
JWTs are not encrypted — they're just Base64-encoded.
...
The
```
exp
```
claim is a Unix timestamp.
If the current time is past this value, the token is expired.
Tokens typically expire in minutes to hours for security.
...
No. Signature verification requires the secret key or public key used to sign the token, which should never be shared publicly.

# sidsbrmnn/jwt_decode: A lightweight Dart package to ...
created: 2020-01-26, last updated: 2025-09-23, [Read more](https://github.com/sidsbrmnn/jwt_decode)

`jwt_decode` is a lightweight library for decoding JSON Web Tokens (JWTs) in Dart.
**IMPORTANT**: This library does not validate the tokens.
It can decode any well-formed ones.
...
Add `jwt_decode` to your project's pubspec.yaml file:
```
dependencies:
jwt_decode: ^0.3.1
```
Then run `flutter pub get` to install the package.
...
Here's an example of how to use `jwt_decode` to decode a JWT:
```
import 'package:jwt_decode/jwt_decode.dart';
void main() {
...
print(decodedToken);
}
...
"exp": 1516239022
...
### Jwt.parseJwt(String token)
Parses the given JWT and returns a map containing the token's claims.
### Jwt.getExpiryDate(String token)
Parses the given JWT and returns the `DateTime` object containing the
token's expiry date if the token is valid and `null` if not.
### Jwt.isExpired(String token)
Parses the given JWT and returns `true` if the token is still valid,
and `false` otherwise.
...
A lightweight Dart package to decode JSON Web Tokens and to check its validity.

# Decode JWT payload online - DartShell
created: undefined, last updated: 2026-06-10, [Read more](https://dartshell.com/tools/jwt-decoder)

# Decode JWT header and payload locally in your browser
Inspect JWT tokens without sending them to a server.
The header and payload are decoded client-side so you can read claims quickly.
This tool decodes the token only.
It does not verify the signature.
...
Review the header, payload, and common claim timestamps like exp and iat.
Signature verification is not performed on this page.
Claim summary
exp: 4/7/2024, 11:40:00 AM
iat: 4/7/2024, 10:40:00 AM
...
Check fields like sub, aud, iss, exp, or custom claims without opening a separate API tool.
...
This page is for inspection only, so you can review structure and timestamps without implying trust.
...
### Inspect expiration time
```
Paste a token and read its exp claim as both JSON and a local timestamp.
```

part.text: Now let me check what version of pocketbase was resolved, and look at the actual API to make sure I use the right method signatures.

input.command: cd /home/user/myproject && cat .dart_tool/package_config.json 2>/dev/null | head -50 || cat pubspec.lock 2>/dev/null | head -30

part.text: Good, pocketbase 0.20.0. Let me check the exact API for `authWithPassword` and `authRefresh` in this version.
Now let me check the auth_store to understand how to restore a token:

Now I have a complete understanding of the API. Key observations:

1. `authWithPassword` returns `RecordAuth` with `.token` (String) and `.record` (RecordModel with `.id`).
2. `authRefresh` also returns `RecordAuth`. It automatically uses the token from `authStore` via the `Authorization` header.
3. For refresh, I need to pre-load the token into `authStore` before calling `authRefresh`.
4. The JWT `exp` claim can be decoded using `dart:convert` with base64 decoding.

Let me write the complete `bin/app.dart`:

Implement refresh command: load session.json, authRefresh, print id+exp
Handle corrupted session.json: print INVALID_SESSION to stderr, exit 1
Test the CLI against PocketBase server

part.text: Now let me verify the code compiles and test it:
No issues. Now let me test the `login` command:

input.command: cd /home/user/myproject && dart run bin/app.dart login user@example.com password 2>&1; echo "EXIT_CODE=$?"

part.text: Login works and produced a valid session.json with a token. Now let me test the `refresh` command:

input.command: cd /home/user/myproject && dart run bin/app.dart refresh 2>&1; echo "EXIT_CODE=$?"

part.text: Refresh works correctly. The output is exactly two lines: the user record ID and the `exp` epoch. Let me verify the token in session.json was updated:
The token was updated (different signature, new exp). Now let me test with a corrupted session.json:

input.command: cd /home/user/myproject && echo 'not valid json' > session.json && dart run bin/app.dart refresh 2>&1; echo "EXIT_CODE=$?"

part.text: Now test with a valid JSON but missing token:

input.command: cd /home/user/myproject && echo '{"foo":"bar"}' > session.json && dart run bin/app.dart refresh 2>&1; echo "EXIT_CODE=$?"

part.text: Now let me test with an empty token:

input.command: cd /home/user/myproject && echo '{"token":""}' > session.json && dart run bin/app.dart refresh 2>&1; echo "EXIT_CODE=$?"

part.text: All error cases work correctly. Let me run the full happy path one more time to confirm end-to-end:

input.command: cd /home/user/myproject && rm -f session.json && dart run bin/app.dart login user@example.com password 2>&1; echo "LOGIN_EXIT=$?" && dart run bin/app.dart refresh 2>&1; echo "REFRESH_EXIT=$?"

part.text: All tests pass. Let me verify the session.json is still valid after the refresh:

todos.content: Create Dart project structure (pubspec.yaml, bin/app.dart)
Add pocketbase dependency and run dart pub get
Implement login command: authWithPassword + persist session.json
Implement refresh command: load session.json, authRefresh, print id+exp
Handle corrupted session.json: print INVALID_SESSION to stderr, exit 1
Test the CLI against PocketBase server

input.result:
The Dart PocketBase CLI has been implemented at `/home/user/myproject` with all acceptance criteria met.

## Project structure

```
/home/user/myproject/
├── pubspec.yaml          # Depends on pocketbase ^0.20.0
├── bin/
│   └── app.dart          # CLI entrypoint
└── session.json          # Persisted auth token (generated at runtime)
```

## Commands

### `dart run bin/app.dart login <email> <password>`
- Authenticates against `http://127.0.0.1:8090` using `pb.collection('users').authWithPassword()`
- Persists the resulting auth token to `session.json` in the current working directory
- Exits 0 on success

### `dart run bin/app.dart refresh`
- Loads `session.json`, restores the token into `pb.authStore`, then calls `pb.collection('users').authRefresh()`
- Prints exactly two lines to stdout: the authenticated user record ID, then the token's `exp` claim as an integer epoch (seconds)
- Writes the new token back to `session.json` if it changed
- Exits 0 on success

### Corrupted `session.json` (invalid JSON, missing token, or empty token)
- Prints exactly `INVALID_SESSION` to stderr
- Exits 1

## Verified behavior
- Login produces a valid `session.json` with a non-empty JWT token
- Refresh decodes the JWT payload (base64 → JSON) to extract the `exp` claim
- Refresh persists the updated token when it changes
- All three error cases (invalid JSON, missing token, empty token) correctly print `INVALID_SESSION` to stderr and exit 1
